entities.description: The immediate printed source of OTA 1213: Reimer's author-focused edition from Add MS 46919, including introduction and apparatus absent from the digital payload and an appendix sermon by another hand absent from the thirty-two-item selection.
entities.label: Stephen R. Reimer, ed., The Works of William Herebert, OFM (1987)
notes: Pritchard describes light emendation of obvious error, supplied missing words and details, modern punctuation, and selective incorporation of marginal or interlinear material.
The digital selection omits the printed introduction, notes, bibliography, indexes, plate, and appendix seventh sermon.

access.format: Onsite consultation subject to access approval

access.accessMethod: No complete public page-image object was identified in the responsible catalog or corroborating manuscript portals.
notes: The BL record's digitised field was blank.
ARLIMA explicitly stated 'Ms. non numérisé' and showed a 2 July 2026 update date.
Biblissima's Mirador interface reported no digitized object; generic IIIF interface boilerplate was not treated as a canvas inventory.
The separately cached f. 205r crop is partial and does not change this complete-facsimile finding.

access.accessMethod: Request complete current-object photography from BL Digitisation Services, naming Add MS 46919 and asking for boards/covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every recto and verso, edges, and detached matter where offered; separately identify the exact selected folios and ask BL to enumerate omissions.
access.format: Quote-dependent photographic TIFFs; request colour explicitly

localCopies.label: British Library Imaging Services rate card 2025–26
notes: The two-page rate-card PDF is completely downloaded; metadata_only describes its coverage relative to manuscript-facsimile access.
The checked rate card listed £27.70 for the first photographic TIFF and £7.25 for each subsequent TIFF.
Original manuscripts require photography; fulfillment and complete-surface coverage are not guaranteed.
No order was placed.

dateStatements.label: Production
dateStatements.notes: Individual portions and hands may have different dates; Sermon 3 is explicitly dated 9 June 1314.
dateStatements.value: England, before 1333?; catalog record date range 1332–1334

entities.description: The sole physical codex evidenced for all thirty-two selected items: a multi-scribe English parchment miscellany containing Herebert's sermons, outlines, and poems among other material.

holding.notes: Current catalog title: William Herebert Collection.

entities.label: London, British Library, Add MS 46919
notes: BL item 48 covers sermons and notes on ff. 159–179 but excepts the other-hand appendix sermon on ff. 163–164v; item 50 covers ff. 181v–184v; item 54 covers f. 204v; item 55 covers nineteen lyrics on ff. 205–211v.
Poems 20–23 occur separately on ff. 161r, 179v, 204v, and 85r.
The main nineteen-poem sequence on ff. 205r–211v is associated with Herefordshire language; the codex as a whole should not be assigned that single localization.
Written by multiple hands; scholarship identifies approximately thirteen hands.
The selected work portion is discontiguous and does not include the entire codex.

rights.basis: The selected sermons, outlines, and poems were created in the early fourteenth century; no modern rights statement is extended to their underlying medieval textual content.
rights.component: Medieval textual content represented by the selected passages
notes: This does not determine rights in modern editions, metadata, photography, or access-delivered reproductions.

next_steps: Ask OTA, U-M, and PIMS to identify which components each statement covers.
Obtain explicit PIMS permission before redistributing modern editorial text beyond a clearly defensible exception.
Keep medieval content, provider encoding, repository serialization, and edition rights separate.
openQuestions.question: How should the OTA CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 provider license be reconciled with the embedded © 1987 PIMS permission statement and U-M No Copyright – United States display?

next_steps: Purchase or consult an authorized copy of the PIMS volume.
Audit each sermon and poem against Reimer's full notes before strengthening item-level claims.
Keep the appendix other-hand sermon outside the digital-selection boundary.
openQuestions.question: What additional source identifications, variants, dates, and attribution qualifications appear in Reimer's complete introduction, notes, and apparatus?

next_steps: Submit a BL Digitisation Services quotation request for the complete current physical object.
Ask for colour TIFFs covering boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf surface, edges, and detached matter where offered.
Ask BL to enumerate omitted or unphotographable surfaces and provide exact file-to-folio mappings.
Record delivery and redistribution terms separately from underlying medieval-text status.
notes: No order was placed during this research task.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete practical-resolution surrogate of the current physical state of Add MS 46919 be obtained and licensed for corpus research?

next_steps: Consult Reimer's complete poem notes, Gneuss 1960, and newer item-level scholarship.
Preserve the BL exception for item vii and DIMEV's competing attribution rather than choosing silently.
Do not infer Poem 23 authorship solely from codex placement.
openQuestions.question: How should current scholarship resolve the authorship of Poems 7, 22, and 23 and distinguish Herebert's English adaptations from Nicholas Bozon's source responsibility?

next_steps: Seek U-M or OTA processing documentation.
Complete a character-level diff that separates structural movement from substantive readings.
Preserve both complete payloads and avoid describing the U-M file as a byte copy.
openQuestions.question: Who performed the March 1996 U-M transformation, when were the small textual interventions introduced, and what specification governed the conversion?

next_steps: Search sermon repertories by incipit and biblical theme.
Check modern hymn and Middle English verse studies for lost, parallel, or derivative witnesses.
Do not treat other manuscripts annotated by Herebert as witnesses without textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: Do item-level repertories or current scholarship establish collateral witnesses for any selected sermon, outline, or poem beyond Add MS 46919?

reviewNotes: The thirty-two selected intellectual items are six sermons, three sermon outlines, and twenty-three poems; group containers are not counted as additional works.
The exact manuscript portion is discontiguous: f. 85r; ff. 159r–162v; f. 164v; ff. 165r–167v; ff. 169r–179v; ff. 181v–184v; f. 204v; and ff. 205r–211v.
The U-M file reorders the OTA payload from poems-sermons-outlines to sermons-outlines-poems and converts legacy markup; it is a version, not a byte-identical copy.
Rights are stated separately for medieval text, edition, deposit, encoding, repository artifact, databases, scholarship, and images.

lineage.summary: The General Corpus Herebert XML is a repository copy of a March 1996 University of Michigan structured encoding derived from Oxford Text Archive deposit 1213. The OTA payload explicitly reproduces Stephen R. Reimer's 1987 PIMS edition of six Latin sermons, three Latin sermon outlines, and twenty-three Middle English poems from British Library Add MS 46919. The cataloged unit is this bounded author-focused selection, not the whole manuscript and not all matter in the printed edition. Reimer's appendix seventh sermon by another hand, printed front matter, notes, and apparatus are excluded from the digital text. The one evidenced physical witness is Add MS 46919; all twenty-three DIMEV poem records point to that codex, with one additional trial-draft occurrence for Poem 13. No complete public codex facsimile was found; one cropped f. 205r image is cached, and the manifest records current British Library consultation and whole-object digitisation routes.

catalogSummary.author: William Herebert, with disputed or unresolved attribution for Poems 7, 22, and 23 and Nicholas Bozon source responsibility for Poem 13
catalogSummary.date: Early fourteenth century, largely before 1333; Sermon 3 was preached 9 June 1314; source edition 1987; OTA deposit 1988
catalogSummary.region: Oxford preaching associations for Sermons 1, 3, and 4; South-West Midlands Middle English, localized to Herefordshire for the main nineteen-poem sequence
catalogSummary.title: The Works of William Herebert, OFM

catalogingSubject.label: The Works of William Herebert, OFM
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is Stephen R. Reimer's author-focused selection as represented by OTA 1213 and the U-M/General Corpus Herebert encoding: six Latin sermons, three Latin sermon outlines, and twenty-three Middle English poems. It is not one medieval work, not the whole multi-scribe miscellany British Library Add MS 46919, not every text written or annotated by Herebert, not Reimer's complete printed volume with introduction, apparatus, bibliography, indexes, and appendix sermon, and not the OTA, U-M, or repository serialization treated merely as a digital artifact.

contentParts.label: Six Latin sermons
contentParts.summary: Six sermons or sermon texts associated with William Herebert, including three explicitly tied to Oxford preaching contexts and one securely dated to 9 June 1314.

contentParts.label: Sermo 1: Dixit Mater Iesu ad eum, Vinum non habent
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.
contentParts.summary: A Latin sermon for the second Sunday after Epiphany on John 2:3, headed as preached in the Church of the Blessed Virgin Mary at Oxford.

contentParts.label: Sermo 2: Vidimus stellam eius in oriente
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.
contentParts.summary: A Latin Epiphany sermon on Matthew 2:2, developing the Magi's coming, adoration, and the conditions of a good hearer.

contentParts.label: Sermo 3: Homo quidam erat dives
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.
contentParts.summary: A Latin sermon for the translation of St Edmund of Canterbury, preached in the Church of the Blessed Virgin Mary at Oxford on Sunday 9 June 1314.

contentParts.label: Sermo 4: Laventur pedes vestri et requiescite
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.
contentParts.summary: A Latin Maundy Thursday sermon headed as preached from the Oxford Franciscan pulpit.

contentParts.label: Sermo 5: Homo cum in honore esset non intellexit
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.
contentParts.summary: A Latin sermon of uncertain liturgical occasion on Psalm 48:13 in the Vulgate numbering.

contentParts.label: Sermo 6: In carne sua stare fecit testamentum
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.
contentParts.summary: A Latin sermon for the Circumcision of the Lord on Ecclesiasticus 44:21.

contentParts.label: Three Latin sermon outlines
contentParts.summary: Three shorter Latin sermon plans: one for St Luke and two for the Assumption of the Virgin.

contentParts.label: Sermon Outline 1: Quatuor facies uni
contentParts.summary: A Latin outline for the feast of St Luke, organized around Ezekiel's four faces.

contentParts.label: Sermon Outline 2: Non est mortua puella, sed dormit
contentParts.summary: A Latin outline for the Assumption of the Virgin on the girl who is not dead but asleep.

contentParts.label: Sermon Outline 3: Introduxit me rex in cellaria sua
contentParts.summary: A Latin outline for the Assumption of the Virgin on Song of Songs 1:3.

contentParts.label: Twenty-three Middle English poems

locators.value: 23 DIMEV records; 24 witness occurrences because record-4895 has a trial draft and final version

notes: The 447 L elements are encoding units, not normalized metrical lines; DIMEV sometimes counts long manuscript or printed lines differently.
Poems 20–23 occur away from the continuous nineteen-poem sequence on ff. 205r–211v.
contentParts.summary: Twenty-three religious and moral poems, chiefly renderings or paraphrases of Latin hymns, antiphons, liturgical texts, and Scripture, with material connected to Nicholas Bozon.

contentParts.label: Poem 1: Hostis Herodes impie
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Herodes, þou wykked fo”.

contentParts.label: Poem 2: Vexilla regis prodeunt
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Þe kynges baneres beth forth ylad”.

contentParts.label: Poem 3: Gloria, Laus, et Honor
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Wele, heriȝyng, and worshype boe to Crist”.

contentParts.label: Poem 4: Popule meus, quid feci tibi?
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “My volk, what habbe y do þe?”.

contentParts.label: Poem 5: Libera me, Domine
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Louerd, shyld me vrom helle deth”.

contentParts.label: Poem 6: Þou wommon boute uére
notes: Fifteen lines from the bottom margin are structurally nested in NOTE1 in U-M but remain part of the 57 encoded L total.
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Þou wommon boute uére”.

contentParts.label: Poem 7: Ave maris stella
notes: The current BL catalog excepts item vii from its general Herebert composition statement; DIMEV names Herebert. Attribution is therefore modeled as disputed.
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Hayl, Leuedy, se-stœrre bryht”.

contentParts.label: Poem 8: Veni Creator Spiritus
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Com, Shuppere, Holy Gost”.

contentParts.label: Poem 9: Alma redemptoris Mater
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Holy moder, þat bere Cryst”.

contentParts.label: Poem 10: Conditor alme siderum
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Holy wrouhte of sterres bryht”.

contentParts.label: Poem 11: Christe redemptor omnium
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Cryst, buggere of alle ycoren”.

contentParts.label: Poem 12: Tu rex glorie Christe
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Þou kyng of wœle and blisse”.

contentParts.label: Poem 13: Vous purueez en cete vye
notes: DIMEV also records a seven-line trial draft on f. 84v after its Anglo-Norman source; the encoded poem follows the final version on ff. 208v–209r.
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Bysœth ȝou in þysylke lyf”.

contentParts.label: Poem 14: Iesu nostra redemptio
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Iesu, oure raunsóun, loue, and lóngynge”.

contentParts.label: Poem 15: Eterne rex altissime
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Kyng hexst of alle kynges”.

contentParts.label: Poem 16: Quis est iste qui venit de Edom?
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “What ys he, þys lordling”.

contentParts.label: Poem 17: Crucem sanctam subiit
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “He sthey opon þe rode”.

contentParts.label: Poem 18: Audi benigne conditor
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Lustne, mylde Wrouhte”.

contentParts.label: Poem 19: Euangelium: Missus est angelus Gabriel
notes: The U-M encoding divides this poem into a prologue and the Missus est text using two nested DIV3 sections.
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Seynt Luk, in hys godspel”.

contentParts.label: Poem 20: Þys nome ys also on honikomb
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Þys nome ys also on honikomb”.

contentParts.label: Poem 21: Hic que uita?
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “What lyf ys þer her?”.

contentParts.label: Poem 22: Quomodo se habet homo?
notes: DIMEV describes the poem as perhaps by William Herebert.
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Also þe lanterne in þe wynd”.

contentParts.label: Poem 23: Vóur þynges ȝe ofte ysœth
notes: DIMEV records the four lines in the lower margin of Herebert's translation of Nicholas Bozon's Contes moralisés; direct authorship remains unresolved.
contentParts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Vóur þynges ȝe ofte ysœth”.

assertion.notes: The range combines OTA cataloging with the witness and Herebert chronology; individual items may have narrower dates.

dateStatements.display: Early fourteenth century, individually unresolved; before 1333 as an outer bound
notes: This is not the copying date of the whole miscellany and not a claim that all thirty-two items were composed together.

scope.description: Date of the Sunday Translation of St Edmund named by the sermon heading

notes: The question mark in the current BL catalog is retained.

scope.description: Attestation of the selected items in Add MS 46919
First documented electronic deposit of the exact thirty-two-item text selection
Navigation-oriented XML transformation represented by the U-M/General Corpus file

extent.description: Thirty-two bounded intellectual items: six sermons, three sermon outlines, and twenty-three poems. The whole-item XML metric is 520 L elements: 447 occur within poem divisions and constitute the intrinsic poem-line extent, while 73 are embedded quotations or verse passages inside prose sermons and do not make those sermons verse works. Five header P are excluded from the 104 body P total. Encoded L counts are not normalized historical metrical-line counts.

formStatements.description: The cataloged selection combines nine prose sermon items with twenty-three verse items.
Six sermons and three outlines are prose, although the sermons contain 73 line-like quotation elements.
The poem divisions contain 129 verse groups and 447 encoded line elements.

scope.description: Latin source titles, rubrics, tags, and retained liturgical phrases within or around the Middle English poems

notes: The Reimer edition's appendix seventh sermon at Add MS 46919 ff. 163r–164v is outside the cataloged thirty-two-item unit even though it is physically adjacent to Herebert's sermons and shares f. 164v with two selected outlines.
Poems 20–23 are part of Reimer's twenty-three-poem edition but are physically discontiguous from the main nineteen-poem sequence.
The exact work-portion folios are f. 85r; ff. 159r–162v; f. 164v; ff. 165r–167v; ff. 169r–179v; ff. 181v–184v; f. 204v; and ff. 205r–211v.

A complete public facsimile of Add MS 46919 was not found as of 2026-07-15; that access finding is recorded in the lineage manifest and is not evidence that private, licensed, or later digitization does not exist.

Rights and access are layer-specific: medieval text, Reimer edition, OTA deposit, U-M presentation, repository artifact, DIMEV data, review images, and manuscript images are not assigned one blanket status.

next_steps: Consult Reimer's complete introduction and poem notes, Gneuss 1960, and current item-level scholarship.
Keep the BL item-vii exception and DIMEV's attribution as conflicting evidence until reconciled.
Do not infer Poem 23 authorship from its codex placement alone.
openQuestions.question: How should current scholarship resolve the authorship of Poems 7, 22, and 23 and the exact Herebert/Bozon relationship for Poem 23?

next_steps: Purchase or consult the PIMS volume or an authorized library copy.
Compare each sermon and poem against Reimer's apparatus before strengthening item-level claims.
Inspect the appendix sermon only as excluded contextual material.
openQuestions.question: What additional source identifications, dates, variants, and editorial qualifications appear in Reimer's inaccessible full introduction, notes, and apparatus?

next_steps: Search sermon repertories by incipit and source theme.
Check modern hymn and Middle English verse scholarship for lost, parallel, or derivative witnesses.
Do not treat other manuscripts annotated by Herebert as witnesses without item-level textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: Do any collateral manuscripts transmit versions of the six sermons, three outlines, or twenty-three poems beyond the one Add MS 46919 witness evidenced here?

next_steps: Seek U-M or OTA processing documentation for the March 1996 edition.
Diff item-normalized OTA and U-M text at character level, separating structural movement from substantive variants.
Preserve both complete payloads and avoid describing U-M as a byte copy.
openQuestions.question: Who performed the U-M navigation transformation, when were the small textual interventions made, and what conversion specification governed them?

notes: Dialect region is not automatically a place of composition.

scope.description: The continuous main sequence on ff. 205r–211v

notes: The scope deliberately excludes Poems 7, 22, and 23, whose attribution requires separate statements.

scope.description: The six sermons, three outlines, and poem items supported as Herebert's in the checked sources

assertion.notes: The BL catalog cites scholarship excepting item vii from its composition claim, whereas DIMEV names Herebert.

notes: Do not flatten the conflict into a certain attribution.
Its placement in Herebert's translation of Bozon does not by itself prove authorship of the four marginal lines.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of Nicholas Bozon material
Anglo-Norman source material rendered by Herebert; not authorship of the Middle English wording
Authorial revisions visible in Add MS 46919
The 1987 source edition and the exact selected contents inherited by the digital texts

summaries.value: An author-focused collection of six Latin sermons, three Latin sermon outlines, and twenty-three Middle English religious and moral poems associated with the Franciscan William Herebert. The sermons display early-fourteenth-century Oxford Franciscan preaching and learning; the poems chiefly render hymns, antiphons, liturgical texts, Scripture, and Nicholas Bozon material into South-West Midlands Middle English. British Library Add MS 46919 preserves the entire selected corpus, much of it in Herebert's hand and with authorial revision.

The exact digital body contains thirty-two intellectual items: six sermons, three sermon outlines, and twenty-three poems. U-M organizes them in that order. OTA 1213 preserves the same items in the order twenty-three poems, six sermons, three outlines. The repository XML has eight DIV1 wrappers, twenty-six DIV2, two nested DIV3, 104 body P, 129 LG, 520 total L, 50 folio milestones, and 48 NOTE1; 447 L belong to poem divisions and 73 to quotations or line-like passages in sermons.

The printed Reimer volume also contains introduction, critical apparatus, commentary, bibliography, indexes, a plate of Add MS 46919 f. 171v, and an appendix seventh sermon in another hand. OTA, U-M, and General Corpus encode the selected work texts and authorial notes but omit the printed front matter and editorial notes; they do not encode the appendix sermon.

All checked source-witness evidence points to one physical codex, British Library Add MS 46919. DIMEV v1.0 identifies all twenty-three poems there; Poem 13 has two occurrences within that same codex, a seven-line trial draft at f. 84v and the final version at ff. 208v–209r. The digital text encodes the final version. Other manuscripts known to contain Herebert annotations are contextual, not evidenced witnesses of these selected texts.

OTA 1213 is a complete 1988 legacy text deposit using Chicago-guide-inspired inline codes. U-M converts those codes to structured XML, reorders the thirty-two items for navigation, promotes folio and occasion comments to milestones and headings, and moves authorial notes inline. Direct comparison confirms the same bounded contents but not byte identity; the precise conversion authority and several small character-level interventions remain undocumented.

The sermons display early-fourteenth-century Oxford Franciscan preaching and learning; the poems chiefly render hymns, antiphons, liturgical texts, Scripture, and Nicholas Bozon material into South-West Midlands Middle English. British Library Add MS 46919 preserves the entire selected corpus, much of it in Herebert's hand and with authorial revision.
